does: This paper documents Netomi's practical engineering approach to deploying large-language-model-based agents in real enterprise settings at scale. The work combines concurrent request handling, formal governance frameworks, and multi-step reasoning chains to achieve reliable production AI workflows using OpenAI's GPT-4.1 and GPT-5.2 models. The core contribution is demonstrating how to maintain system stability, audit trails, and business logic compliance while processing high volumes of agent requests in mission-critical enterprise environments where failures propagate directly to customers.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in autonomous agent architectures and tool-using language models, extending prior research on prompt engineering and chain-of-thought reasoning into practical production systems. It responds to the gap between prototype-stage agent research and the governance and concurrency challenges that emerge in enterprise deployment. The paper connects to work on language model reliability, reasoning with external tools, and the growing literature on production ML systems, while addressing specific pain points from previous attempts to scale agents in regulated business contexts.
enables: By establishing patterns for enterprise-grade agent deployment, this work opens pathways for more ambitious agentic applications in industries with strict compliance and reliability requirements. It provides a foundation for future research into automated governance frameworks for AI agents, scaling approaches that maintain auditability, and methods for decomposing complex business workflows into reliable multi-agent systems. The documented challenges and solutions enable the field to move beyond single-agent research prototypes toward frameworks for building trustworthy, production-ready autonomous systems in high-stakes commercial settings.
